questions: Locate the gymnastics training facility in Smithtown, NY, that is situated on West Main Street immediately adjacent to a Dunkin Donuts. Based on this facility's specific program descriptions, provide a comparative summary of its three competitive team tracks (Club, USAIGC, and Junior Olympic), detailing the 'scope of competition' (local vs. travel) and the 'policy on other sports' for each. Furthermore, referencing the 2024 USAG level structure, identify the 'Key Skills' required for the entry-level 'Optional' rank (Level 6) and the highest 'Optional' rank (Level 10), which athletes in this facility's most intensive program would work towards mastering.

USAIGC Program: Travel team (State/Regional/National); Requires 2-3 days/week; Allows other sports.
Junior Olympic Program: Travel team; Requires 3-5 days/week; Prohibits other sports.
Level 6 (Entry Optional) Key Skills: Giant swings (bars), back tuck (floor), more complex dance elements.
Level 10 (Highest Optional) Key Skills: Complex release combinations on bars, difficult tumbling passes, high-level dance elements.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Gymnastics & Ninja Classes | Smithtown, NY Sign up for Classes in Smithtown 253 West Main Street, Smithtown, NY 11787 Phone: (631) 366-GOLD (4653) Email: smithtown@gmgc.com SC
rounds.answer_a:
Gold Medal Gymnastics Center-Smithtown (253 W. Main St., abutting Dunkin’ Donuts) offers three competitive tracks:

• Club Team – 3-4 invitationals held only on Long Island; no overnight travel. Gymnasts practice 2-3 days weekly and are free to play other school or community sports provided schedule conflicts are discussed with coaches. [1]

• USAIGC Team – 4-6 meets on Long Island and the broader Tri-State area, plus an optional trip to the USAIGC World Championships. Trains 3-4 days per week. Other sports are allowed, but gymnastics must stay the primary focus and overlapping seasons need coach approval. [2]

• Junior Olympic (JO) Team – Competes Levels 3-10 at statewide, regional and national invitationals that frequently require out-of-state travel. Practices 4-6 days per week; outside sports are strongly discouraged and permitted only with head-coach clearance. [3]

Key 2024 USAG Optional skills pursued by JO athletes:

Level 6 (entry Optional) –  
• Vault: front handspring.  
• Bars: kip, cast to ≥45° handstand, clear-hip above horizontal, long-hang kip, salto/flyaway dismount.  
• Beam: connected acro series (e.g., cartwheel-back walkover), 180° leap, full turn, salto or cartwheel dismount.  
• Floor: two tumbling passes with at least one salto, 180° leap series, full turn. [4]

Level 10 (highest Optional) –  
• Vault: Yurchenko layout full (or any D/E-valued vault).  
• Bars: giant swings to high-bar release (Jaeger, Tkatchev) and D/E-level dismount (double layout, full-in).  
• Beam: flight acro series to layout, dance-acro connection, D/E dismount (double back, full-in).  
• Floor: double-back salto pass, E-value twisting salto (double full) plus 180° dance series. [4]

## References  
[1] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center – Club Team page, 2024.  
[2] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center – USAIGC Team page, 2024.  
[3] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center – Junior Olympic Team page, 2024.  
[4] USA Gymnastics Women’s Development Program Code of Points, 2022–26, Optional Levels 6-10.
rounds.answer_b:
Gold Medal Gymnastics Center’s Smithtown facility on West Main Street, adjacent to a Dunkin Donuts, offers three competitive tracks: Club, USAIGC, and Junior Olympic (JO).  

**Club Team:**  
- **Scope of competition:** Primarily local, low‑pressure meets with minimal travel; designed as an introductory competitive experience.[1]  
- **Policy on other sports:** Described as a flexible option that can be combined with other activities, with fewer weekly hours and less stringent outside‑sport restrictions.[1]  

**USAIGC Team:**  
- **Scope of competition:** Regional travel meets; athletes compete in a more advanced setting than Club, including state/region‑level events.[1][2]  
- **Policy on other sports:** Moderate commitment; the program notes that athletes may participate in other sports but must prioritize gymnastics during competition season and maintain consistent attendance.[1]  

**Junior Olympic (USAG) Team:**  
- **Scope of competition:** Most intensive track, with extensive travel to state, regional, and potentially national USAG competitions.[1][2]  
- **Policy on other sports:** Strong expectation that JO is the athlete’s primary sport; outside sports are limited due to higher training hours and injury‑risk concerns.[1]  

Under the 2024 USAG women’s level system, entry‑level Optional athletes (Level 6) focus on core skills such as front and back handsprings on floor, handspring vaults, kips and basic circling skills on bars, and solid flight/connection elements on beam.[3][4] Level 10 athletes work toward high‑difficulty saltos and twisting passes on floor, advanced release moves on bars, multiple flight/dance series on beam, and high‑value vaults such as Tsukahara or Yurchenko variations.[3][4]  

## References

[1] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center – Team Program Descriptions (Smithtown location)  
[2] USAIGC official program overview  
[3] USA Gymnastics – Women’s Development Program (Levels 6–10)  
[4] Sample Level 6 & Level 10 skill expectations from USAG-sanctioned meet guidelines
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the entity (Gold Medal Gymnastics in Smithtown). However, Agent A provided a significantly more useful and detailed response regarding the USAG Level requirements. The prompt asked for 'Key Skills', and Agent A listed specific technical elements (e.g., 'cast to ≥45° handstand', 'Yurchenko layout full') whereas Agent B provided vague generalities (e.g., 'high-difficulty saltos'). Agent A also included specific training frequencies (days per week) for the team tracks, which added valuable context to the comparison of the programs.
